title: Jeremiah's call, almond rod, and boiling pot
summary_text: Jeremiah is called from the womb and appointed as a prophet over nations and kingdoms. The Lord shows him an almond rod and a boiling pot to mark judgment from the north, then promises to strengthen him against kings, priests, and the people. The chapter establishes both the authority of the word and the cost of speaking it.
key_points: Jeremiah is appointed before birth to uproot and to build.
The almond rod and boiling pot signal coming judgment.
God makes the prophet like a fortified city.
Fear of people must not silence God's message.

title: Forsaking the fountain of living waters
summary_text: The Lord remembers Israel's early devotion but charges the nation with abandoning him for broken cisterns. Priests, prophets, and leaders all share blame because they have not asked where the Lord is and have trusted in useless gods. The chapter frames backsliding as both spiritual adultery and self-destruction.
key_points: Israel has exchanged the fountain of living waters for cracked cisterns.
The nation has not asked where the Lord is.
Foreign alliances and idols will not save them.
Sin makes the people the cause of their own ruin.
evidence_phrases: you have said, I am innocent

title: Return, faithless Israel
summary_text: The Lord uses marriage and adultery imagery to show how deeply Israel has wandered, yet he still invites her to return. Judah has seen the warning in Israel's exile but has copied the same sin, so the call to repentance becomes urgent for both kingdoms. The chapter ends with a promise of shepherds, honest confession, and healing for backsliding children.
key_points: The Lord still calls faithless Israel to return.
Judah should have learned from Israel's exile but did not.
Repentance requires honest confession, not performance.
God promises shepherds after the heart he desires.
evidence_phrases: we lie down in our shame

title: Repentance, invasion, and a devastated land
summary_text: Jeremiah calls Judah to return with an honest heart before the destroyer from the north arrives. The chapter then widens into a vision of a land stripped of peace, beauty, and life because the people have not turned from evil. The prophet's grief over the coming disaster is itself part of the warning.
key_points: If the people return, the disaster can be averted.
The north will bring a terrifying invasion.
The land is portrayed as empty and mourning.
The prophet's lament matches the nation's coming sorrow.
evidence_phrases: the whole land shall be desolate

title: No righteous man in Jerusalem
summary_text: Jeremiah searches the streets for even one who does justice and truth, but both the poor and the powerful have broken faith. The people swear falsely, refuse correction, and chase after lust and deceit, so the city cannot stand. Judgment is delayed only until the Lord brings the full account against them.
key_points: No one in Jerusalem is found seeking truth.
The leaders are as corrupt as the poor.
The Lord will visit the city for its rebellion.
Sin has become so normal that it no longer shocks.
evidence_phrases: they love to have it so

title: Siege, refusal, and rejected word
summary_text: The trumpet sounds alarm against Jerusalem because the enemy is at the gate and the city's beauty will be stripped away. Yet the people reject the Lord's word, delighting in their own ways even as the prophet pleads for them to listen. The chapter shows how stubborn refusal turns warning into certainty.
key_points: Judgment is announced with the sound of a trumpet.
The people refuse to hear the Lord's correction.
Religious practice without obedience is worthless.
The city's own stubbornness makes the siege unavoidable.

title: The temple sermon and false trust
summary_text: At the temple gate Jeremiah warns the worshipers that the building itself cannot save a people who oppress the weak and worship other gods. He recalls Shiloh as a warning that God's dwelling place can be judged when the covenant is broken. The chapter turns sacred space into a test of obedience rather than a guarantee of protection.
key_points: The temple is not a shield for unrepentant sin.
God cares about justice, mercy, and covenant faithfulness.
Shiloh proves that holy places can be judged.
The people must stop trusting in lies and return to obedience.

title: Stubborn people and false peace
summary_text: Jeremiah laments that the people know the law but do not act like the law matters, so even the wise and the scribes are trapped in deception. He mourns over a harvest that has passed with no healing and no peace, because the nation keeps saying what is not true. The chapter is a funeral song for a people who refuse correction.
key_points: Knowledge without obedience is useless.
False prophets heal lightly and speak peace when there is none.
The harvest has passed but the wounds remain.
Jeremiah's grief reveals the people's real condition.
evidence_phrases: is there no balm in Gilead

title: The prophet's lament over deceit and tears
summary_text: The prophet wishes for tears because the land is full of deceit, violence, and neighbor betrayal. The Lord explains that these sins are not trivial habits but covenant-breaking practices that bring bitter judgment. The chapter closes with the call to boast only in understanding and knowing the Lord.
key_points: Jeremiah grieves because truth has vanished from the land.
The people are skilled in lies and corruption.
God's judgment fits the moral rot he exposes.
The only rightful boast is knowing the Lord.
evidence_phrases: oh that my head were waters
they bend their tongue like their bow
let not the wise man glory

title: Idols are nothing; the Lord is the living God
summary_text: Jeremiah contrasts handmade idols with the living God who made the heavens and governs the nations. The false gods are mute, unstable, and carried by people, while the Lord alone thunderously acts in history. The chapter also prays for correction, asking God to discipline in justice rather than destroy.
key_points: Idols cannot move, speak, or save themselves.
The Lord made the earth and the heavens.
Jeremiah asks for correction in judgment, not wrath.
Human fear of idols should give way to awe before God.
evidence_phrases: the Lord is the true God

title: The broken covenant and the conspiracy against Jeremiah
summary_text: Jeremiah repeats the covenant terms of Sinai, then announces that Judah has broken them and brought the curse on itself. The people of Anathoth and Judah conspire against him for speaking God's words, but the Lord reveals their plot and promises judgment. The chapter joins covenant indictment with personal persecution.
key_points: The covenant curse falls because the people broke God's terms.
Jeremiah's own hometown turns against him.
God sees the hidden plot and judges the conspirators.
Prophetic faithfulness often brings personal danger.
evidence_phrases: hear the words of this covenant

title: Jeremiah's complaint about the prosperity of the wicked
summary_text: Jeremiah asks why the wicked prosper even though he has obeyed God and suffered for it. The Lord answers by warning that greater trials are coming and by teaching Jeremiah not to confuse easy living with final favor. The chapter pushes the prophet toward patience in a world where justice seems delayed.
key_points: The prophet is honest about the apparent success of evil people.
God does not deny the complaint but deepens the lesson.
Jeremiah must learn endurance before more severe testing.
The Lord will still judge those who have harmed his inheritance.
evidence_phrases: how long shall the land mourn
if you have run with the footmen

title: The linen belt and the ruined pride of Judah
summary_text: Jeremiah's hidden linen belt becomes a sign that Judah was meant to cling to the Lord but has instead become worthless through pride. The ruined wineskins then picture a nation that will be filled with confusion and exposed in its shame. The chapter warns that arrogance always ends in disgrace when the Lord refuses to hide it.
key_points: The linen belt was meant to be useful but has become ruined.
Pride leads to public humiliation.
The king, queen, and people will be carried away.
Jerusalem's shame is the result of forgetting the Lord.

title: The drought and the plea of false prophets
summary_text: A severe drought exposes the land's fragility and the people's guilt, while even kings and commoners search for water. Jeremiah confesses the sin of the nation and begs for mercy, but the Lord warns that false prophets are speaking lies in his name. The chapter shows both physical dryness and spiritual deceit.
key_points: The drought is a sign of deeper covenant failure.
False prophets promise peace without divine authority.
The people are asked to confess their sin before God.
Neither sacrifice nor optimism can replace repentance.

title: Jeremiah's grief and the Lord's reply
summary_text: The Lord says even Moses and Samuel would not sway him, because Judah's sin has reached the point of no return. Jeremiah responds with personal lament, asking why his pain feels endless when he has spoken for God faithfully. The chapter ties the nation's ruin to the prophet's own suffering, showing how deeply he shares the message he carries.
key_points: Judah's sin has crossed a line that prayer cannot erase.
Jeremiah feels the cost of his calling personally.
The Lord promises both sword and captivity.
The prophet's pain is not a mistake but part of the burden.
evidence_phrases: though Moses and Samuel stood before me
I sat not in the assembly

title: No family, no feasting, and future return
summary_text: Jeremiah is told not to marry or participate in public mourning or celebration, because the coming disaster will consume the land. Those restrictions become a living sign that the normal rhythms of life are about to break. Yet the chapter also looks ahead to a day when the Lord will bring his people back from far countries.
key_points: Jeremiah's lonely life is itself a warning.
Joy and grief both stop because judgment is near.
The land is filled with idols and unfaithfulness.
The Lord will still gather his people from exile.
evidence_phrases: you shall not take a wife
out of the land of the north

title: Sin engraved on the heart and Sabbath trust
summary_text: Judah's sin is written as if with an iron pen on the heart, making its guilt impossible to hide. The Lord contrasts cursed trust in man with blessed trust in himself, and he calls the people to honor the Sabbath as a sign of that trust. The chapter moves from inward corruption to outward obedience.
key_points: Sin is deeply engraved and not superficial.
Trusting people instead of God leads to barrenness.
The Sabbath reveals whether the heart is listening.
God's blessing rests on those who make him their confidence.
evidence_phrases: written with a pen of iron
bear no burden on the Sabbath

title: The potter's house and the rejected message
summary_text: At the potter's house Jeremiah sees that the Lord can reshape a nation as easily as clay in the hand of a craftsman. That lesson is immediately resisted, because the people insist on their own plans and plot against the prophet. The chapter shows both God's sovereignty and the nation's stubborn refusal to be remade.
key_points: God has the right to remold a nation that turns evil.
A threatened people often answer with more rebellion.
Jeremiah is rejected for speaking plainly.
The potter image teaches humility before divine authority.
evidence_phrases: as the clay in the potter's hand
remember that I stood before you

title: The broken flask in the valley of Hinnom
summary_text: Jeremiah breaks an earthen flask in the valley of Hinnom to picture the irreversible shattering of Judah and Jerusalem. The place of child sacrifice becomes the setting for a public declaration that judgment will be as final as shattered pottery. The sign-act makes the prophecy impossible to ignore.
key_points: The broken flask shows a nation beyond easy repair.
Topheth becomes a place of judgment for bloodshed.
Child sacrifice is singled out as a terrible sin.
What is shattered in God's hand is shattered for a reason.
evidence_phrases: the valley of the son of Hinnom

title: Pashhur strikes Jeremiah and hears his name change
summary_text: Pashhur beats Jeremiah and puts him in stocks, but the prophet answers with a new name for the priest: terror on every side. The suffering does not silence Jeremiah; instead it sharpens his lament and his confidence that the Lord will vindicate him. The chapter makes public humiliation part of prophetic witness.
key_points: Jeremiah is abused for speaking the word.
The priest's false security is named and judged.
Jeremiah complains honestly about the cost of his calling.
He still trusts that the Lord sees and repays.

title: Jerusalem must surrender to Babylon
summary_text: Zedekiah asks Jeremiah for rescue, but the answer is that Babylon will not be stopped and Jerusalem must surrender or die. The Lord himself is fighting against the city because its long rebellion has reached its end. The message is stark: the only path left is submission to God's sentence.
key_points: Jerusalem cannot be saved by last-minute panic.
The Lord has turned against the city in judgment.
Choosing surrender is the only way to survive.
Kings and warriors are no match for divine decree.
evidence_phrases: I have set my face against this city

title: Judgment on the kings of Judah
summary_text: Jeremiah addresses the royal house directly, demanding justice, compassion, and obedience from the throne. Each king is measured against the covenant rather than his building projects or military confidence, and the chapter ends by exposing pride as the reason for downfall. The throne is only stable when it serves righteousness.
key_points: Kings are judged by how they treat the vulnerable.
Luxury and cedar do not prove divine favor.
Shame and exile await the proud rulers.
A throne without justice is doomed.
evidence_phrases: they shall not lament for him

title: False shepherds and the righteous Branch
summary_text: The Lord condemns shepherds who scatter the flock and speak visions from their own hearts instead of from his mouth. Against those lies he promises the righteous Branch from David, a king whose name will be 'The Lord our righteousness.' The chapter contrasts corrupt voices with true prophetic word and a true king.
key_points: Bad shepherds scatter rather than gather the flock.
The Lord sees the lies of prophets who speak from themselves.
A Davidic Branch will rule in justice and safety.
The true word of God is not like a dream born of deceit.
evidence_phrases: Is not my word like fire

title: Good figs and bad figs
summary_text: Jeremiah sees two baskets of figs, one good and one rotten, as a picture of the exiles and the remaining leaders. The good figs represent those sent away who will one day be restored, while the bad figs stand for those who stayed behind in stubborn rebellion. The vision turns exile into a sign of hope for those willing to be disciplined.
key_points: The vision sorts people by their response to God's discipline.
Exiles may be the ones whom God preserves for good.
The remaining leaders are compared to rotten fruit.
Judgment and hope are both present in the same sign.
evidence_phrases: I will regard them for good

title: Seventy years and the cup of wrath
summary_text: Jeremiah explains that the nation's long refusal to listen has brought the Babylonian captivity and a cup of wrath that will spill to many nations. The seventy years are not random; they are the measured discipline of God over Judah and the surrounding world. The chapter widens judgment from one people to a global drinking of divine punishment.
key_points: Judah has ignored repeated prophetic warning.
Babylon's rise serves God's disciplinary purpose.
All the nations will drink from the cup of wrath.
The discipline has a measured end point.
evidence_phrases: the cup of the wine of wrath
the Lord roars from on high

title: Temple warning, trial, and Jeremiah spared
summary_text: Jeremiah repeats the temple warning and is nearly executed for it, but priests, princes, and elders hear his defense. The trial recalls earlier prophets and shows that speaking truth in the Lord's name is not the same as speaking against the nation. Jeremiah is spared, even though another prophet is killed for the same message.
key_points: The temple sermon is repeated despite the risk.
Jeremiah's defense rests on God's sending.
A prior prophet's death underscores the danger.
Truth is spared only when God grants mercy.

title: The yoke of Babylon
summary_text: Jeremiah wears a yoke to show that the nations must submit to Babylon until God decides otherwise. Envoys and prophets who encourage rebellion are warned that resistance will only bring sword, famine, and pestilence. The message is not political optimism but obedience to the Lord's present decree.
key_points: The yoke is a sign that God has given Babylon this role.
National pride cannot cancel divine discipline.
False prophets make resistance sound holy when it is not.
Submission is safer than pretending the judgment is not real.
evidence_phrases: I have given all these lands

title: Hananiah breaks the yoke and proves false
summary_text: Hananiah publicly breaks Jeremiah's yoke and promises a quick end to Babylon's rule, but the Lord rejects his optimism as a lie. Jeremiah answers that wooden yokes can be replaced by iron, and Hananiah dies that same year as a sign of false prophecy's end. The chapter contrasts smooth predictions with the hard truth of God's word.
key_points: Public confidence is not the same as divine authorization.
Breaking the sign does not break the message.
The Lord replaces easy promises with heavier discipline.
False peace ends in death and shame.
evidence_phrases: you have broken the yokes of wood

title: A letter to the exiles in Babylon
summary_text: Jeremiah writes to the exiles, telling them to build homes, plant gardens, seek the city's peace, and wait patiently for the Lord's timing. The letter rejects the prophets who promise immediate return and warns that rebellion will bring loss even in exile. The chapter also records a promise that those who seek the Lord will be found and restored.
key_points: Exile is long enough to require ordinary faithfulness.
The city of Babylon is not home, but it must still be lived in wisely.
False prophets will not speed God's timetable.
Seeking the Lord remains the path to future hope.
evidence_phrases: build houses and dwell in them
seek the peace of the city

title: The book of comfort and the return from distress
summary_text: Jeremiah records a promise that the days of distress will end and Israel will return to worship and security. The Lord describes the pain of exile as a labor that leads to deliverance, and he promises to break the oppressor's yoke. The chapter shifts from warning to hope without denying the severity of the present wound.
key_points: The Lord sees the anguish of his people.
Exile is not the final word.
The oppressor's yoke will be broken.
Restoration will come after trouble.

title: The new covenant and Rachel's weeping
summary_text: The Lord promises to regather Israel with love, to turn mourning into comfort, and to plant the people in security. Rachel weeps for her children, but the grief is answered by the promise of return and the greatest covenant renewal in the book. The new covenant writes the law on the heart and makes forgiveness the defining feature of God's relation to his people.
key_points: The Lord loves Israel with everlasting love.
Rachel's grief is answered by hope for her children.
The new covenant moves the law from stone to heart.
Forgiveness becomes the center of restored relationship.
evidence_phrases: I have loved you with an everlasting love
I will put my law in their inward parts

title: Jeremiah buys a field during the siege
summary_text: While Jerusalem is under siege, Jeremiah buys a field in Anathoth as a public sign that life will continue after judgment. The purchase looks irrational in the moment, but it is a concrete act of hope in God's promise of restoration. The chapter joins legal paperwork, prayer, and prophetic certainty.
key_points: Buying land during siege is a deliberate act of faith.
The Lord promises houses, fields, and vineyards again.
Jeremiah's prayer wrestles with God's power and goodness.
The sign matters because exile will not last forever.

title: A renewed promise of Davidic peace
summary_text: The Lord speaks again in prison and promises healing, rebuilding, and a Davidic branch that will execute justice and righteousness. Joy returns to Jerusalem because the covenant with David and Levi is not forgotten even in ruin. The chapter gathers restoration around the certainty of God's own name and word.
key_points: God can speak hope even from prison.
The Davidic promise still stands.
The city will again hear the voice of joy.
The Lord will keep his covenant with the priesthood.

title: Freedom for the slaves and judgment on Zedekiah
summary_text: Zedekiah is told that he will not escape Babylon, while the people who briefly free their Hebrew slaves reverse course and become guilty again. The Lord treats the broken emancipation as covenant treachery and sends judgment accordingly. The chapter shows that outward reform without obedience is still rebellion.
key_points: The king will face Babylon rather than escape it.
Freeing slaves and then reclaiming them is covenant betrayal.
God judges broken promises as seriously as open rebellion.
Delayed obedience is not obedience.
evidence_phrases: they turned and caused them to return
I will give them into the hand

title: The Rechabites keep their father's command
summary_text: Jeremiah sets the Rechabites before the nation as a living contrast: they obey their ancestor's command while Judah ignores the Lord. Their discipline, sobriety, and fidelity shame the people who have rejected a far greater voice. The chapter uses family loyalty to expose covenant disloyalty.
key_points: The Rechabites obey a human command consistently.
Judah refuses the Lord's word again and again.
Faithfulness can be seen in ordinary habits.
The Lord honors the Rechabites' obedience.
evidence_phrases: a man to stand before me

title: The scroll burned and written again
summary_text: Jeremiah dictates the Lord's words to Baruch, who reads them publicly until the king cuts and burns the scroll piece by piece. The destruction of the written message only leads to a rewritten scroll with more words, showing that human fire cannot silence divine speech. The chapter is a dramatic picture of resisting revelation.
key_points: The scroll preserves the spoken word for public hearing.
The king burns what he refuses to hear.
God simply commands the message to be written again.
Human contempt does not cancel revelation.
evidence_phrases: read in the ears of the people

title: Zedekiah consults Jeremiah and hears the same word
summary_text: During a temporary lull in the siege, Zedekiah asks Jeremiah for prayer, but he does not change his course or trust the message. Jeremiah is arrested as a defector, then rescued, and he repeats that Babylon's outcome will not be reversed by wishful thinking. The chapter shows a king who wants relief without surrender.
key_points: A king can ask for prayer and still refuse obedience.
Jeremiah's message remains unchanged under pressure.
False accusations cannot erase the word of God.
The prophet is sustained even in confinement.

title: Jeremiah in the cistern and rescued by Ebed-melech
summary_text: Jeremiah is cast into a muddy cistern for discouraging the city, but Ebed-melech intercedes and helps pull him out. Zedekiah secretly asks the prophet what will happen and hears the same answer: surrender to Babylon and live. The chapter pairs brutality with mercy and keeps the word intact through both.
key_points: Faithful speech can land a prophet in a pit.
A foreign official becomes the instrument of rescue.
The king receives a private answer that still demands obedience.
Surrender remains the only live path.

title: Jerusalem falls and Zedekiah is captured
summary_text: The city is breached, Zedekiah flees, and Babylon captures him after his sons are killed before his eyes. Jerusalem falls just as Jeremiah said, and the final devastation confirms the seriousness of the warnings that were rejected. The chapter is the book's grim turning point, where prophecy becomes history.
key_points: Jerusalem's defenses finally collapse.
The king's escape fails and his line ends in ruin.
The Lord's word is shown to be exact.
Judgment arrives after long refusal to listen.

title: Jeremiah released to Gedaliah
summary_text: Jeremiah is released after the fall and allowed to live among the remnant under Gedaliah's governorship. The chapter shifts from siege to the fragile beginning of life in the ruined land, with a warning that the future still depends on listening to the Lord. The mercy shown to Jeremiah contrasts with the nation's collapse.
key_points: Jeremiah is freed from the chain of captivity.
Gedaliah is placed over the people left in the land.
The remnant must choose whether to live by faith or fear.
A ruined nation still has a moral future.

title: Ishmael murders Gedaliah
summary_text: Ishmael murders Gedaliah and the people with him, then attempts to carry the remnant away by force. The violence shatters the fragile order that had begun after Jerusalem's fall. The chapter shows that political collapse creates room for greed, fear, and betrayal.
key_points: Gedaliah's murder destroys the first attempt at stability.
The remnant is again exposed to violence and fear.
Ishmael's deception reveals the depth of the crisis.
Leadership failure leads to renewed displacement.
evidence_phrases: ten men in the midst of the city

title: The remnant asks for counsel and hears a warning
summary_text: The remnant asks Jeremiah to pray for guidance, promising obedience no matter what the answer is. God tells them not to go to Egypt, because the same sword and famine they fear in Judah will follow them there if they disobey. The chapter tests whether the survivors really mean what they say.
key_points: The people ask for prayer but already want a preferred answer.
God's counsel is clear: stay in the land.
Egypt is not a safe refuge from disobedience.
Promises made under pressure are tested by action.
evidence_phrases: whether it is good or bad
do not be afraid of the king of Babylon

title: The remnant flees to Egypt against the word
summary_text: When Jeremiah warns against Egypt, the people accuse him of lying and drag the whole remnant south anyway. In Egypt the Lord again speaks through Jeremiah, showing that distance does not cancel his authority. The chapter makes disobedience into a journey rather than a single choice.
key_points: The people reject the warning and go where they wish.
Relocating does not remove God's voice.
Jeremiah continues to speak in the new place.
Fear can push a remnant into deeper rebellion.

title: The rebuke of idolatry in Egypt
summary_text: In Egypt Jeremiah confronts the people who keep burning incense to other gods and pretending that the disaster came from elsewhere. The Lord answers by repeating the logic of their history: idolatry brought ruin in Judah and will do the same in Egypt. The chapter closes with a final contest between covenant loyalty and stubborn apostasy.
key_points: The people in Egypt continue the same sins that ruined Judah.
The prophet explains that judgment followed idolatry before.
The Lord will not be mocked by repeated rebellion.
Refusal to change is itself a judgment.

title: A word to Baruch
summary_text: Baruch is discouraged, and the Lord tells him not to seek great things for himself because disaster is already coming on the land. The promise is not prestige but survival, so long as he receives his life as a gift. The chapter gives a brief personal word inside a book of national collapse.
key_points: Baruch is warned not to chase status in a collapsing time.
God's answer is preservation, not promotion.
The disaster is broad enough to swallow ambition.
Faithfulness matters more than advancement.
evidence_phrases: I will give you your life
the evil that I will bring

title: Judgment on Egypt
summary_text: Jeremiah announces Egypt's defeat at Carchemish and describes the nation as a proud power that cannot stand before Babylon. The oracle broadens into a picture of panic, retreat, and the humiliation of a once-mighty empire. Yet God also warns his people not to fear the right-sized judgment because he will later deal with every nation.
key_points: Egypt's military confidence collapses at Carchemish.
The Lord names the defeat before it happens.
Panic replaces the empire's former boasting.
No nation stands above God's judgment.

title: Judgment on the Philistines
summary_text: Before the fall of Gaza, Jeremiah foretells that the Philistines will be swept away by an unstoppable flood of judgment. The image is swift and devastating, showing that even longstanding enemies are not beyond God's reach. The chapter is brief but intense, like the nation it condemns.
key_points: Judgment arrives from the north like a rising flood.
The sword of the Lord reaches Gaza and its cities.
Human enemies are not hidden from divine timing.
A short oracle can still carry complete judgment.

title: Judgment on Moab
summary_text: Moab is stripped of pride, with its towns, crops, and worship centers all brought low. The chapter lingers over the nation's grief and shame, then closes with a promise that Moab's former confidence will not save it. The oracle is one of Jeremiah's most extended poems of national collapse.
key_points: Moab's pride is repeatedly named as its weakness.
The nation loses both prosperity and refuge.
Its god cannot rescue it from judgment.
Grief runs through the oracle from beginning to end.

title: Oracles against Ammon, Edom, Damascus, Kedar, Elam, and others
summary_text: Jeremiah pronounces a series of judgments on surrounding nations, showing that the Lord's rule extends far beyond Judah. Each oracle names a different pride, violence, or false security, and each one ends with the Lord's hand visible in history. The chapter is a map of geopolitical collapse under divine sovereignty.
key_points: No neighboring nation is exempt from God's rule.
Each oracle matches a specific national sin.
The Lord can scatter, terrify, and restore as he chooses.
Every prideful stronghold is temporary.

title: Judgment on Babylon
summary_text: The chapter announces Babylon's collapse and calls God's people to leave the doomed city when the time comes. The proud empire that wielded the sword against others will itself be hunted down and exposed. The message combines vengeance, rescue, and the promise that Israel's Redeemer will not forget his people.
key_points: Babylon's defeat is certain and public.
God tells his people to flee the collapsing city.
The empire that hunted others will be hunted.
Redemption and judgment are linked in the same oracle.
evidence_phrases: flee out of the midst of Babylon

title: Babylon's downfall and the scroll in the Euphrates
summary_text: Jeremiah continues the judgment against Babylon, describing the city as a golden cup that has made the nations drunk. He sends a scroll with Seraiah to be read and sunk in the Euphrates, symbolizing Babylon's final plunge. The chapter turns poetic judgment into a physical sign that the empire will not rise again.
key_points: Babylon's greatness is part of its guilt.
The scroll sinking in the river signs the city's end.
The nations are summoned to see the Lord's vengeance.
What God writes against Babylon will stand.
evidence_phrases: Babylon has been a golden cup

title: The fall of Jerusalem and the exile ledger
summary_text: The book closes by repeating Jerusalem's fall, Zedekiah's capture, the temple's destruction, and the carrying away of the people. The final verses list exiled groups and the release of Jehoiachin, preserving the historical aftermath with meticulous care. The ending is grim but factual, leaving the reader with the reality of judgment and a faint thread of royal survival.
key_points: Jerusalem's destruction is recorded in detail.
The temple and palace are stripped and burned.
The exile continues as a census of loss.
Jehoiachin's release gives a tiny sign of continuation.
